title: US hits dozens of countries with new wave of tariffs
description: The US has imposed new tariffs on 60 trading partners in the latest escalation in the trade war reignited by US President Donald Trump when he returne
body: The US has imposed new tariffs on 60 trading partners in the latest escalation in the trade war reignited by US President Donald Trump when he returned to office last year.Those targeted, including the UK, China and the European Union, will face a tariff of 10% to 12.5% on all goods, accounting for almost all American imports.The tariffs, which replace an identical levy which expires on Friday, are based on claims key economic partners have failed to properly tackle forced labour.But US trade expert Caroline Freund said the move is "not about forced labour" but that Trump is simply "looking for a legal reason to put the tariffs in".The US Supreme Court had ruled earlier this year that many of the tariffs imposed globally under emergency powers were illegally enacted.The 10%-12.5% duties on imports were proposed last month by the White House and over concerns they were not doing enough to tackle forced labour."Today's action will begin to correct what is both a human rights abuse and distortive trade practice to improve the welfare of workers everywhere," US Trade Representative Jamieson Greer said.The new duties apply to the top 60 US trade partners covering 99.4% of US imports, it added.The new tariffs were a "like for like" swap with the regime that expired on Friday, Freund, Dean of the UC San Diego School of Global Policy and Strategy, told the BBC's Today programme.What tariffs has Trump introduced and why?Asked about the forced labour justification, she said: "I think they were looking for a legal reason to put the tariffs in and that they can maintain them because their goals and Greer has been very clear about this, as has Trump, is about the trade deficit and it is about US manufacturing, it is not about forced labour."The new levies show the Trump administration is "determined" to push on with its tariff strategy, said trade policy expert Deborah Elms from the Hinrich Foundation.It is unlikely countries hit with tariffs will be able to prove that they have sufficient measures to prevent forced labour imports, she told the BBC.The levies are likely to raise costs for businesses and consumers, although its impact could be softened due to the number of exempted goods, said the Asia Society Policy Institute's economic security expert Wendy Cutler.She added most trading partners would be disappointed with the new levies and were likely to focus on ways to "reduce their dependence on the US" by making deals with other countries.The head of the British Chambers of Commerce (BCC) said the UK had lost its comparative advantage against the European Union as a result of the new tariffs.William Bain told the BBC the EU has a 10% all-inclusive deal for tariffs on its goods, while the UK is facing 10% universal tariffs on top of any duties imposed on individual goods."So there will be some concerns in the business community this morning about what the UK needs to do to get the same treatment the European Union has got here," Bain added.David Henig, director of UK trade policy at the European Centre for International Political Economy, also noted the UK's relative disadvantage compared with the EU.He told Today: "We have slightly moved backwards, but this is President Trump so anything could change tomorrow or the day after."I don't think too many businesses will be changing their plans based on that."The UK government said firms were facing no changes to the tariff rate they are facing."We take forced labour very seriously to ensure that in global supply chains UK businesses are not complicit," a government spokesperson said.And UK whisky will not be affected by the new tariffs, after Trump struck a deal to exempt the tipple during King Charles and Queen Camilla's state visit to the US.Other countries have criticised the announcement, with Brazil's government calling the new 12.5% rate it is facing "unjustified".The Japanese government on Friday said it regretted the new tariffs, while Australian Trade Minister Don Farrell said the levies were "completely unjustified".China has previously said it opposed any form of unilateral tariff, and denied allegations of forced labour."There is no so-called forced labour in China, and we oppose using this as an excuse for political manipulation," Chinese foreign ministry spokesperson Mao Ning said.But several international human rights groups have said forced labour does exist in China, particularly among Muslim ethnic minorities in Xinjiang.

schema.headline: US hits dozens of countries with new wave of tariffs

title: US and Iran trade more strikes as fears of escalation mount
description: Iran says it has carried out drone strikes on US military facilities and bases across the Middle East in retaliation for the 13th consecutive night of
body: Iran says it has carried out drone strikes on US military facilities and bases across the Middle East in retaliation for the 13th consecutive night of American attacks on the country.Explosions were reported along Iran's Gulf coast, and in western, northern and central areas. Four people were killed and five injured in an attack in the south-west, Iranian state media said.Iran later announced strikes on US-allied Kuwait, while sirens sounded in Bahrain and Jordan, and flights were suspended at a major Iraqi airport after a reported drone crash.It comes as Iran condemned the US plan to use frozen Iranian assets to pay for war damages, in a sign of further escalation between the two countries.On Thursday, US President Donald Trump said damages to ships and cargo in the Strait of Hormuz would be paid for by "Iranian Money that the United States has in its possession, and controls"."These damages may be very substantial but, nevertheless, this is the fair and equitable thing to do," he posted on his Truth Social platform.Iranian Foreign Minister Abbas Araghchi responded by saying that seizing another country's assets to "pay for unrelated future claims is an incendiary precedent"."Ensuing chaos will not be pretty or peaceful," he added.In June, the US and Iran agreed on a 14-point Memorandum of Understanding (MOU) to halt military operations and reopen the Strait of Hormuz, as well as to reach an agreement to end the war over the following 60 days.But three weeks after it was signed, Trump declared the ceasefire "over" after Iranian attacks on ships in the strait, and US strikes in response.Point 11 of the MoU detailed that the "United States of America undertakes to make fully available for use the frozen or restricted funds and assets of the Islamic Republic of Iran".As the US pushes to regain control over the Strait of Hormuz, there's growing concern over new threats to shipping in the Red Sea from Yemen's Iran-backed Houthis.That has helped push the price of crude oil back over $100 (£74.96) a barrel - the highest level since May.Trump separately on Thursday vowed "major military punishment" against Iran for Houthi attacks.He later told the Axios news website he was considering restarting major combat operations in Iran and launching strikes "bigger than ever before". Tensions have been escalating between the US and Iran since the start of July.On Thursday night, the US military says it carried out strikes to degrade Iran's ability to threaten civilian shipping in regional waters. US Central Command (Centcom) said it had targeted "Iranian military command centers, drone storage facilities, communication networks, coastal surveillance sites and maritime capabilities".Iran's army claimed it had launched attacks on US bases in Kuwait, while Bahrain's Interior Ministry reported that its air sirens had been sounded and Iranian state media also said alarms had been activated in Jordan.The AFP and Reuters news agencies both reported a drone crash at Iraq's Erbil International Airport, causing a temporary stoppage. Flights have since resumed.

schema.headline: US and Iran trade more strikes as fears of escalation mount

title: Content creator who left bathtub on Pen y Fan apologises
description: A content creator who took a bathtub up a mountain and left it there has apologised for what he called an "honest mistake".Tom Christopher, 25, posted
body: A content creator who took a bathtub up a mountain and left it there has apologised for what he called an "honest mistake".Tom Christopher, 25, posted footage online of him carrying the tub up Pen y Fan, in Bannau Brycheiniog National Park - also known as the Brecon Beacons - in flip-flops during hot weather on 17 July, before bathing in it at the summit.Christopher, from Swansea, said he had planned to take it back down immediately, but people had asked him to keep it there so they could take pictures - sparking a backlash about its impact on the environment.A day later Cardiff Rugby academy players found the tub while doing volunteering work, and handed it to park officials.Christopher said he wanted to make a "cool, wholesome video which made people smile" by carrying the bath up Pen y Fan, which stands 2,907ft (886m) above sea level."It was a complete, honest mistake," Christopher said."I obviously didn't mistakenly leave it up there, I didn't forget. It was a mistake in the fact I thought I was doing the right thing with people telling me it was 'awesome' and 'cool'. "I should never have left anything up the mountain. I'm glad the message of this story is that, even though I'm the villain, everyone's sticking up for the national park. "It's a dirty mistake, I shouldn't be leaving anything up the mountain. "It's my fault for thinking it was a good idea, when clearly I should have been thinking more about keeping our nature clean," he said.After being urged by people to leave it there, he said he had planned to go back two days later and send it back down on wheels.He said he had subsequently received many messages criticising him for leaving it there.Christopher said he was told the incident had been reported as fly-tipping, and he was unclear if he would be fined."I haven't got the money. I'm in my overdraft, I haven't got the money to do anything."If it comes, I'll be gutted. I hope there's an option to do some voluntary work to try and pay the fine."Cardiff Rugby academy players stumbled on Christopher's bathtub while carrying out essential maintenance work on the mountain, and helped rangers retrieve it.Writing on Facebook, the academy described it as an "additional and unexpected team-building exercise".A spokesperson for National Trust Cymru said its rangers helped the rugby players bring the bathtub down "due to the risk it posed to both visitors and the site of scientific interest [SSSI] with the potential for contamination".Bannau Brycheiniog National Park said its team work hard to maintain the site's natural beauty, biodiversity and access, adding that dealing with waste and litter left by visitors takes considerable staff resources away from this vital work. The park said littering and fly-tipping were a significant problem across Bannau Brycheiniog - impacting wildlife, livestock and local communities - and encouraged everyone who visits the National Park to act responsibly and leave no trace.

schema.headline: Content creator who left bathtub on Pen y Fan apologises

title: Burnham to spend first day working from No 10 North
description: Andy Burnham will hold his first meeting at the new No 10 North in Manchester later on Friday.The branch of Downing Street in the North West will form
body: Andy Burnham will hold his first meeting at the new No 10 North in Manchester later on Friday.The branch of Downing Street in the North West will form part of the prime minister's aim to shift power from Whitehall and Westminster to regions across the country.Ministers Lucy Powell and Louise Haigh will also operate regularly from the new site, with up to 300 civil servants expected to join them by the end of next year.On Friday in Manchester, Burnham will chair a meeting of the National Economic Council, a cabinet committee first launched in 2008 to respond to economic challenges around the UK.The meeting will bring together senior ministers and regional mayors to "put devolution at the centre of the government's mission to make Britain better".Ahead of that meeting, the prime minister said: "For 40 years, power and resources have been sucked into the centre, and too many communities have felt forgotten, without the attention and investment they deserve."He added: "The days of Whitehall resisting devolution are over, for good."Strategic decisions about devolution and local economic growth will shift from the Treasury and the Ministry of Housing, Communities and Local Government to No 10 North, although both departments will have input in these areas.Angela Rayner, whose constituency is in Greater Manchester, is also expected to work from the new site for the first time on Friday.Asked whether Burnham's messaging was too focused on the north of England, Rayner told BBC Breakfast people understood No 10 North was about driving growth in every postcode across the UK.Burnham's first day as prime minister in No 10 North will include talks with business leaders, including representatives of the Federation of Small Business, the Confederation of British Industry and the British Chambers of Commerce, about how to generate growth and investment. Labour wants to create a plan that would pull from existing devolution strategy and local growth policy, to allow civil servants, mayors, councils and businesses to work together more closely.Burnham's first week in power has seen him make a series of interventions aimed at easing the cost of living, including announcing a 20% cut in business rates for pubs, social clubs and live music venues from April next year.However, Conservative leader Kemi Badenoch has criticised his ambitions for being "too small".Speaking on Thursday, she urged him to tackle high energy costs for businesses as well as a gap in defence funding."When you are mayor of Manchester, you are handed a big pot of someone else's money, and you decide where to spend it," she said."I'm sure this is great fun. It can make you very popular. But it is not the same as being prime minister."Andy Burnham needs to do better. He is behaving like he's still the mayor of Manchester. He is now the prime minister of the United Kingdom, he needs to start acting like it."

schema.headline: Burnham to spend first day working from No 10 North

title: Chelsea transfer news: Maxence Lacroix deal agreed with Crystal Palace
description: Crystal Palace defender Maxence Lacroix is set to join Chelsea on a long-term contract after the clubs reached agreement on a deal for the player.BBC
body: Crystal Palace defender Maxence Lacroix is set to join Chelsea on a long-term contract after the clubs reached agreement on a deal for the player.BBC Sport reported on Tuesday that both clubs had held positive talks over a move for the France international, with Palace seeking about £60m for the centre-back.An agreement has now been reached between the two clubs, with personal terms not believed to be an issue.Lacroix is expected to undergo a medical in the next 24 hours before signing a long-term contract, understood to run until 2032.The agreement between the two London clubs has been negotiated at ownership level.The 26-year-old was a regular under Oliver Glasner last season, making 55 appearances in all competitions as Palace won the Uefa Conference League, the club's first major European trophy.He also featured in Palace's Community Shield victory over Liverpool and helped the Eagles secure another strong Premier League campaign.Lacroix's performances earned him a place in France's squad for the 2026 World Cup. He made three appearances during the tournament, including starts against Norway and England in the third-place play-off, as Les Bleus finished fourth.Palace signed Lacroix from Bundesliga side Wolfsburg in August 2024 for an initial fee of £15.5m, with a further £2.6m in potential add-ons.He has gone on to establish himself as one of the Premier League's most consistent central defenders.

schema.headline: Chelsea transfer news: Maxence Lacroix deal agreed with Crystal Palace

title: Fifty women tell BBC they were pressured into having sex with strangers
description: Image caption, Many of the woman spoke of long-lasting traumaByCatrin NyeAdam Eley and Divya TalwarBBC News InvestigationsWarning: This story contains
body: Image caption, Many of the woman spoke of long-lasting traumaByCatrin NyeAdam Eley and Divya TalwarBBC News InvestigationsWarning: This story contains details of sexual actsFifty women have told the BBC they were pressured or coerced by their partners into having sex with strangers.They got in contact following an investigation which told the story of a woman who said she had been pressured into having sex with more than 100 people she did not know.Many of the women who came forward said they had sex through swinging despite not wanting to, either because they feared the consequences of refusing or felt they had to keep their partners happy.They spoke of deep, long-lasting trauma."I remember many nights of my partner keeping me up all night screaming at me, threatening me, and attempting to gaslight me into believing this was something I wanted," said one woman. "It was never what I wanted."Another told us: "I felt like I had been given away. I felt used. I felt so dirty."Most encounters were arranged via the website FabSwingers, say the women, which claims to have more than half a million UK active monthly accounts.Six women told BBC News they had been raped by men met through the site.One said her partner had forced her to have sex with more than 90 men through FabSwingers. On one occasion, he held her down by the ankles, she told us.Sixteen said intimate photos of them had been uploaded to the website without their permission. SwingersA detailed and at times disturbing investigation raising questions about the idea of consent and whether abuse can exist behind the language of sexual freedom.Swinging typically involves couples meeting up and exchanging partners, but it can also involve just one half of a couple having sexual encounters while their partner watches. Many people take part fully consensually.Some of the women who got in touch accuse FabSwingers of facilitating abuse and failing to tackle illegal behaviour.The site says it has moderation policies and is not "responsible for private off-platform conduct between adults"."We do not accept any suggestion that FabSwingers is indifferent to misuse," it added.But the former minister for safeguarding and violence against women and girls, Labour MP Jess Phillips, believes FabSwingers "should be shut down".Police should recognise coercion or pressure to take part in swinging as a form of adult sexual exploitation, she says. "Calling it swinging implies consent, and therefore puts the blame at the feet of the women, and that makes it silencing for those women."Experts have told BBC News it is important to understand that people can be manipulated into consent. Partners who pressure women into swinging can then use the resulting shame as a means of control, they say.Image caption, Ruth says she continues to suffer flashbacksThe Swingers podcast and our previous article told the story of Ruth O'Grady, who said her ex-husband would arrange for her to have sex with other men, sometimes as many as four in a single day. She says she went along with it to keep him happy. Her ex-husband has not responded to BBC News's questions.The backgrounds and relationships of the women who contacted us in response to Ruth's story are all different - but many said her honesty had given them permission to talk about their own abuse, kept hidden for years.One woman said, had she not seen our reporting, she "probably would have had sex against my will literally this evening. This has been my situation for 12 years now." The abuse will take place "no more", she said.Another woman, Emily from Derbyshire, said she had been pushed into going to a swinging club while pregnant. "That was my baby girl," she said of her daughter. "It feels awful that I mixed her up in it."Emily had hoped a single visit would be enough, but she said her husband wanted more, and they ended up joining FabSwingers. For years, evenings became consumed by searching the site for hook-ups.If their young daughter had not fallen asleep by 7pm, Emily said her husband would come into the bedroom and pull her away saying: "Mummy's got to go now."Emily said her daughter would cry because she was not ready to say goodnight, or because they had not finished the bedtime story.She eventually left the marriage but estimates she had sex with between 20 and 30 men while swinging.She did not think of it as sexual abuse at the time, she told us, because she had agreed to try swinging. She does now.Afraid to go to the policeSixteen users told us they had reported to police either alleged abuse or the posting of intimate images on FabSwingers without consent. Three said this had led to convictions.However, 10 raised concerns about the way police had handled their cases.One woman said she was 21 when her boyfriend put her on FabSwingers. He ran the whole profile, she told us, and she does not even know the identity of some of the men she had sex with because her boyfriend would make sure she could not see them or talk to them.She went to the police but they eventually dropped her case. She says they told her she looked like she had been enjoying the sex in the videos they had viewed.The woman said she explained to officers that her ex-boyfriend would be angry if she did not look enthusiastic enough and make her have sex with someone else."The police made me feel like I was disgusting," she said.Details of organisations offering information about or support after sexual abuse or with feelings of despair are available at bbc.co.uk/actionlinePolice need better training to understand coercive control and how pressure to engage in sexual acts can form part of domestic abuse, says Dame Nicole Jacobs, domestic abuse commissioner for England and Wales.The National Police Chiefs Council told the BBC: "Victims deserve to be listened to and supported consistently across the country."One woman who contacted us said she felt she could not go to the police because her husband was a serving police officer.Susie, whose name we have changed, said he talked for years about his fantasy of her having sex with other men - and eventually persuaded her to join FabSwingers.She described one meet where her husband sat on a sofa in a hotel room and just watched her have sex with a stranger - a practice known as "hotwifing".The next man her husband wanted her to have sex with was a fellow police officer he worked with, said Susie.The whole swinging experience broke her, she told us: "I wasn't sleeping. I was drinking so much every night." She is now getting divorced and taking antidepressants.Image caption, The investigation prompted discussion on FabSwingers internal message boardsEleven men also got in touch with their experiences of having used FabSwingers.One described arriving at a house for sex, arranged through the website, and finding a woman naked on a bed. A phone was "propped up on the side and a male voice was telling her what to do next", explained the man.He and the woman then had sex, he said, and the man at the end of line "wanted evidence of it, by me holding the phone up close to show him". The woman "had no say on what went down at all".It was only when he listened to the Swingers podcast, that he considered she was potentially being controlled and abused, he said. He now regrets what happened.'They don't care about women's safety'Nine users told the BBC they had reported abuse to FabSwingers without any response, including one man who had reported the sexual assault of his female partner by a user.Platforms like FabSwingers must, under the Online Safety Act, remove illegal content - including the non-consensual sharing of intimate images. They must also reduce risks of harm, including controlling and coercive behaviour.Three women told us FabSwingers had failed to co-operate with police investigations - while multiple users said they had faced repercussions for reporting wrongdoing, including being banned from chat forums where they could flag their concerns.The website does not care about women's safety at all, said one woman, yet it "constantly protects dangerous men".FabSwingers told us that reports of coercion, sexual assault and the non-consensual sharing of intimate images are prioritised. It said it works with police and does not penalise those reporting concerns.Three former site moderators told us, however, that none of them had been given any training. Two said they had worked in return for free membership - while the third said they had worked voluntarily.Image source, Getty ImagesImage caption, MP Jess Phillips says FabSwingers should be shut downThis is concerning, Jess Phillips told us: "Not even training your moderators on your website. They should be ashamed of themselves."Unlike some other swinging sites, FabSwingers does not require identity verification, which can make it harder for police to identify suspects when allegations are reported. It also means a banned user can create a new account - although FabSwingers says it can detect this, without explaining how it does so.FabSwingers told the BBC its moderation processes involve up to five site administrators, who moderators report to, alongside AI and "automated controls".Since 2023, the website has been mentioned in at least 329 police reports across the UK - according to Freedom of Information requests submitted by the BBC - including in allegations of rape, sexual offences, coercive control, harassment, blackmail, stalking, assault and possession of extreme pornography. Although, it does not mean FabSwingers was responsible for what was alleged in the reports.Jess Phillips said she believes the site "falls foul" of the Online Safety Act, adding she would encourage the regulator Ofcom "to look into it very, very seriously right now and shut it down".Ofcom says it is "extremely concerned" to hear of the BBC's findings and is speaking to the site's provider, but it has not so far launched an investigation.The vast majority of those contacting the BBC were women who wanted to thank Ruth O'Grady, the subject of the Swingers podcast, for sharing her story."I'm still not in a place where I want anyone I love to know some of these things about my past,"  one woman told us. "But I feel a little less alone now."If you would like to contact BBC Journalist Catrin Nye and the Swingers investigation team about this story you can email on swingersinvestigation@bbc.co.uk, externalAdditional research by Emily Parsons

schema.headline: Fifty women tell BBC they were pressured into having sex with strangers

title: They became best friends – then discovered they were brother and sister
description: Dave Green and Andrea Urmston found they instantly hit it off when their paths crossed while working in separate roles at Manchester Airport.The two s
body: Dave Green and Andrea Urmston found they instantly hit it off when their paths crossed while working in separate roles at Manchester Airport.The two strangers got on so well they could complete each other's sentences - which made sense when they discovered the truth. Dave was Andrea's biological brother, but had been adopted by another family when he was a baby."I was shaking, I was just shaking and my heart was pounding," Andrea told the BBC. "I'd finally found him. It's unbelievable."In 2005, Dave was managing the bus station at the airport and Andrea serviced the vending machines.Over the next two years they became firm friends, texting and phoning each other when they were not at work.One day during a phone call, Dave told Andrea something he had never mentioned before. He was adopted and his birth name was Stuart Proudlove."I kind of froze and didn't speak for a minute," said Andrea. Her maiden name was Proudlove and she had a brother who was adopted before she was born."It's not a very popular name and it had to be him. Other than family, I had never heard that surname anywhere else," she added.She immediately rang her mother, Susan, to check her long-lost brother's birth date. They matched: 24 March 1966.  She called Dave straight back."I said 'it's got to be you, it's got to be you', and I said 'I need to see you', and so I went to pick him up," Andrea said.As they were driving to their mother's house, Dave realised where he was. They were driving along the bus route he used to cover before becoming a manager.His old route had taken him right past his mother's house, and the school that Andrea had attended.He had been within touching distance of his birth family without realising.Susan said finally meeting Dave was a surreal moment. "There were lots of tears. Then we just all hugged," said Andrea."I couldn't believe that I'd got my brother and my mum together, it was amazing."Andrea's mother had told her that she had a brother when she was younger.She told her he had been given up for adoption and had gone to a lovely lady who could not have children of her own."I immediately wanted to go and call him so we could play," Andrea said. Susan said she had tried to find her son with the help of the Salvation Army, but had not got anywhere. "I asked if we could go and get him back," Andrea said. "She did explain that it didn't work that way, so I promised her that I would find him for her one day."Now they were finally being reunited."She had wanted that since being as young as I can remember," Anderea added."So yeah, I gave her that.  I promised her that."On the mantlepiece in Susan's home was a picture of Dave as a baby.It had been sent by his adopted parents to his birth mother days after his adoption.The same photograph had also been displayed in his adopted parents' house.Dave had known he was adopted since he was 10 years old because his adopted parents had shown him the paperwork.But he said he had a very happy childhood, and had never harboured any desire to track down his birth mother."I didn't feel like I had to go searching or have any unanswered questions," he added. For all three, finding each other has been a life-changing experience."I went from having really not a lot of positivity in my life at the time, to finding out that my best friend was my sister," Dave said."I'd found my birth mum being able to sort of make her truly happy. And I'd found a family really that I never really knew existed."Their mother died recently, but both said they were glad she got to be reunited with her son."She was so desperate to find you to make sure you'd had a really good life," Andrea said.For Andrea, the reunion was bittersweet. She had longed to find her brother for years."My mum and dad, they separated unfortunately and so I went through a lot of hard times knowing [Dave] was out there but I didn't know [where]."The pair said they were now making the most of the time they have together after moving in with each other in Kidsgrove, Staffordshire. Finding each other was, they said, their "lottery win".

schema.headline: They became best friends – then discovered they were brother and sister

title: Heatwave anxiety can make you feel 'incredibly helpless' - here's how to manage it
description: At the peak of the June heatwave, the warmest June on record in England, all Tavia Watts could think about was how hot it was in her two- and five-yea
body: At the peak of the June heatwave, the warmest June on record in England, all Tavia Watts could think about was how hot it was in her two- and five-year-old's bedroom - above 30C in some cases."During the heatwave, I've got a high level of anxiety all the time and I'm pretty much useless," she told the BBC. "I've had to leave bedtime when my husband reads [my kids] stories to have a little cry and to calm down, because it's just too hot in their room and I'm afraid for them."Feeling "incredibly helpless", the 36-year-old posted a short reel to her hundreds of followers on Instagram, in which she voiced her inner anxieties. To her surprise, it got more than 18,000 likes and 1,700 reposts.In the video Tavia, a stay-at-home mum living in Cheltenham, says the heat "feels like the world is literally ending", she's "terrified for [her] children's lives", and that she "wants to scream at the people not taking this seriously".Watts is not alone. A 2022 study from the University of Bath estimates that about 4.6% of people in the UK have climate anxiety, which is recognised as a form of mental distress by professional bodies and the NHS. Young people and women are among the groups most at risk, according to a study published in 2025."Anticipatory anxiety", related to heatwaves in particular, has become more common, say psychologists Dr Caroline Hickman from the University of Bath and Dr Emma Lawrance from the University of Oxford."Once people have been through a traumatic event like a flood or a heatwave, they anticipate the next one," Hickman, who offers therapy to individuals experiencing climate anxiety, told the BBC.In the UK, "now we're getting the repeat, repeat, repeat [heatwaves]. It triggers that sense that 'I can't be safe in the world, and the world can't be trusted'," she added.In June's heatwave, Hickman said she "had people saying they're going to kill themselves, I've had people saying they're going to terminate pregnancies, I've had people saying that they want to sell their houses and move somewhere cooler".Watts said that her video "clearly struck a chord with people" - including therapists posting comments about coping mechanisms - and that she wanted to help others deal with climate anxiety."It can go one of two ways. You can either just fall into doomism and think, 'What is the point?'" she said."Or you can try to do something. I sign petitions during a heatwave; I organise climate community events; I run a free children's clothes swap shop. During the last heatwave, I finally became vegetarian, because then I can tell my kids at least I tried."Lawrance had some recommendations on how to cope with climate anxiety:Talk to others: There's often "a huge perception gap between how concerned people are about climate change, and how much they think other people care", said Lawrance - meaning you might wrongly assume that you're in the minority, or even that you're alone."Make space to be kind to yourself, to tend to those emotions and know that it's coming from a place of care for the world," she added.Find a community: Lawrance recommended finding a strong social network who care about the same things, and taking positive action together. Whether it's planting a garden in your school or putting solar panels on the roof of your business, action can not only help you channel your anxiety into something positive, but can help alleviate the symptoms of anxiety too.Lawrance cited the Climate Psychology Alliance in the UK for further support, as well as groups like the Resilience Project, the Climate Cares Centre, and the Climate Majority Project.Keep yourself cool: Your physical health also affects your mental health, and being hot can also make your anxiety and mood worse, Lawrance said - so she recommended making sure to keep yourself physically cool in extreme heat."Having a cool shower, putting a cold washer on your neck, making sure you're hydrated - all of those things will help physiologically as well," she said. "There's a lot we can do - we're not powerless."

schema.headline: Heatwave anxiety can make you feel 'incredibly helpless' - here's how to manage it

title: We split bills equally even when one of us earned a lot more
description: Hannah and Max have always split their money equally even when one has earned considerably more than the other. "We've always wanted to do things 50/5
body: Hannah and Max have always split their money equally even when one has earned considerably more than the other. "We've always wanted to do things 50/50," Max, 31, says. "We made a commitment to each other that what's mine is yours, and vice versa."The couple got married after two years together, when Max was earning £70,000 a year working in tech, and Hannah, who works in the charity sector, was earning considerably less.Their salaries go into a joint account and their mortgage, bills, food, petrol and other household costs are paid out of this.Each month they take the same amount for personal spending.If one of them wants to buy clothes, make-up or go to the pub they can do that without feeling watched and "it means we aren't accountable to each other for those things", says Max.They also divide money into different pots, including savings and smaller funds for predictable spending like haircuts.The couple's system of sharing money remained the same even when Max was made redundant last year and they had to rely on Hannah's salary of between £40,000 and £60,000. "There was shock and panic initially," Hannah, who did not want to disclose her precise salary, says. "But we sat down and really looked through where we could cut things out."They cancelled gym memberships and Sky TV and went through their finances to make sure they could cover their mortgage and bills on a lower income.Max had trained at culinary school instead of doing A-levels and university, but had moved away from hospitality because he felt it would not pay enough.When he was made redundant he wanted to use the opportunity to create his own business in something he genuinely enjoyed - but Hannah had reservations."At first I told him to set this up alongside getting another job but we quickly realised the business would take a lot of time and energy," she says."We ran the numbers and decided that this was the right moment to take the risk given we don't have kids and no one else is depending on us."Max spent £20,000 from his redundancy package buying and converting a horsebox trailer into a pizza truck, which now caters for weddings, parties and pop-ups.He says the money he invested was their agreed limit to make the business work and if it failed he would find another job rather than putting in more.The business now brings in between £4,000 and £6,000 a month in revenue and Max pays himself a salary that covers the mortgage.

schema.headline: We split bills equally even when one of us earned a lot more

title: World Cup 2026: Jurgen Klopp must sway doubters in Germany rebuild
description: Messiah or papering over cracks? Klopp must sway doubters in Germany rebuildImage source, Getty ImagesImage caption, Klopp has signed a four-year deal
body: Messiah or papering over cracks? Klopp must sway doubters in Germany rebuildImage source, Getty ImagesImage caption, Klopp has signed a four-year deal as Germany head coachByKit HoldenGerman football writerHe was once hailed as Liverpool's footballing messiah. Now Jurgen Klopp has been asked to work miracles for his country.Last month, Klopp watched on as a touchline TV pundit as Germany went out of the World Cup in a shock penalty shootout defeat by Paraguay.The loss led head coach Julian Nagelsmann to resign and plunged German football into a full-scale identity crisis.Just three weeks later, it is Klopp who has been tasked with picking up the pieces and restoring his country's once-famous winning mentality, signing a four-year deal to become head coach.Assigned with leading the team through Euro 2028 and the 2030 World Cup, Klopp's first game in charge will be against the Netherlands on 24 September."The national team can unite us Germans like almost nothing else. That's precisely what makes this task so special for me," Klopp said.A Champions League and Premier League winner with Liverpool and a two-time Bundesliga champion with Borussia Dortmund, the 59-year-old is already considered one of the greatest German managers.Yet while many believe he is the perfect man for the national team job, the appointment has also sparked fierce debate in Germany.For the German football association (DFB), securing Klopp's signature has been a priority since Nagelsmann resigned in the wake of the Paraguay defeat.DFB president Bernd Neuendorf said Klopp "embodies passion, credibility, and the ability to inspire people". "After Julian Nagelsmann's resignation, he was our unequivocal choice for the position of national team coach. That's why we pursued him relentlessly," Neuendorf added.Now the deal is done.Image source, Getty ImagesImage caption, Klopp has worked as a pundit on German television during the 2026 World CupKlopp has signed a deal until 2030 and will build his coaching team around former Liverpool assistants Pep Lijnders and Peter Krawietz.Germany will hope the trio can do for them what they once did for the Premier League club and restore one of football's fallen giants to its former glory.Few doubt Klopp has what it takes to breathe new life into the national team.In a recent poll by Germany's leading football magazine Kicker, external, 80% of readers said they thought Klopp was the right choice.Of the many criticisms of the German team in recent years, the most enduring have been a lack of winning mentality and a failure to bond with fans.Klopp, who has spent his career talking about "mentality monsters" and became a cult hero at every club he has managed, would seem to be the perfect solution.Speaking on the popular weekly football talk show Doppelpass, former Germany and Bayern Munich midfielder Stefan Effenberg said Klopp could bring a "new spirit of optimism".For Borussia Dortmund chief executive Carsten Cramer, the new manager's influence may even go beyond sport."I think he embodies a lot of the things that our football and our country are missing right now," Cramer told Kicker.'Messiah' Klopp is not loved by allYet not everyone is so enamoured of the new national team boss.Klopp's reputation has suffered in Germany since he joined Red Bull as head of global soccer in 2024, in a move many fans saw as a betrayal.German fans have long criticised Red Bull for its multi-club model and effective circumvention of the much-loved 50+1 rule, which guarantees supporter voting rights at German clubs.In July, reports Klopp could continue to work as a brand ambassador for Red Bull after becoming Germany manager were met with outrage.Former DFB president Theo Zwanziger told Frankfurter Allgemeine newspaper, external such a move would be "an absolute no-go", adding Red Bull "want to win power inside the sport".Meanwhile, fan group Unsere Kurve said the DFB should not "kowtow to capital" by allowing Red Bull to keep Klopp on their books."Before hailing him as the Messiah, we should remember how much goodwill he has squandered, especially among Dortmund fans," the group's spokesperson Thomas Kessen told news agency SID.Others have criticised the DFB's rush to hire Klopp without considering other candidates."It's obvious that he is number one, but when you see the delegation flying to New York, it seems a bit hectic. You have to ask: do they have any alternatives?" said 2014 World Cup winner Bastian Schweinsteiger as a pundit on ARD.He added Klopp could only be a "band-aid", as Germany's current woes run much deeper than the dugout.Klopp cannot rebuild German football aloneThe German men's national team is currently at its lowest ebb in decades, having failed to reach the last 16 at the past three World Cups.Many, including Klopp himself, have argued for root-and-branch reform at all levels of the game."I have said many times that it's not just about the head coach," the incoming Germany manager told MagentaTV earlier this month."German football as a whole is facing a turning point, we have to change some fundamental things."For Klopp, the rebuild will begin in September when Germany open their Nations League campaign against the Netherlands, their traditional rivals.The real test for the 59-year-old will come in two years' time, when the UK and Ireland host Euro 2028.In a twist of fate, that tournament could see Klopp make an emotional return to the city of Liverpool.Though Anfield is not a venue at Euro 2028, Klopp's Germany could end up playing at Everton's Hill Dickinson Stadium.By that time, we may have a better idea of whether Liverpool's saviour has been able to work his wonders with Germany.

schema.headline: World Cup 2026: Jurgen Klopp must sway doubters in Germany rebuild

title: CJP protests at Jantar Mantar: The youth movement India's Modi didn't see coming
description: Image source, AFP via Getty ImagesImage caption, A Cockroach Janta Party (CJP) supporter wearing a cockroach mask at a protest over irregularities in
body: Image source, AFP via Getty ImagesImage caption, A Cockroach Janta Party (CJP) supporter wearing a cockroach mask at a protest over irregularities in major public examinations in DelhiByIndia correspondentIt began with a fear familiar to millions of young Indians: if the examinations that determine their future cannot be trusted, what can?Weeks later, on Monday, tens of thousands of young men and women tried to march on India's parliament. Police responded with tear gas and baton charges. Thousands more have returned to the streets in the days since.The Cockroach Janta Party (CJP), a youth-led movement born out of anger over repeated examination paper leaks, has quickly become India's biggest student mobilisation in years. It is demanding education reforms and the resignation of Education Minister Dharmendra Pradhan. At its centre is activist Sonam Wangchuk, who says he lost 11kg (24lb) during a 26-day hunger strike in support of the movement. He ended his fast late on Thursday, but student leaders say the protests will continue until their demands are met.The obvious question is whether this is simply another student protest - or the beginning of something larger.History offers reasons for both caution and optimism.Student movements have periodically transformed Indian politics. The Nav Nirman agitation in Gujarat helped trigger veteran socialist leader Jayaprakash Narayan's movement against former prime minister Indira Gandhi in the 1970s.  Others have erupted dramatically before fading away.Image source, ReutersImage caption, A protester hurls a tear gas canister back at police during a CJP march in DelhiWhat makes the CJP unusual, however, is not simply its scale but the nature and breadth of its grievance.Unlike many of the biggest protests against Prime Minister Narendra Modi's government over the past decade - whether over citizenship, farm laws or other contentious policies - this movement is not fundamentally about ideology. Nor is it rooted in the interests of a single community or social group.Instead, it questions whether the state can still guarantee fairness in the examination system that millions of young Indians see as their principal route to social mobility."The exam and education system cuts across caste, class and gender to quite a large degree," Yamini Aiyar, Senior Visiting Fellow at Brown University,  told the BBC. "The core constituency coming to these protests doesn't represent a singular interest group."That breadth is also what makes the movement politically distinctive."This movement centres on administrative failure," Rahul Verma, a fellow at the Centre for Policy Research, a Delhi-based think tank, told the BBC. "The state's inability to conduct examinations fairly and prevent paper leaks makes it a very different kind of political challenge to the BJP."Why are thousands protesting for education reform in India?The hunger-striking engineer demanding education reforms in IndiaThat difference, he argues, changes the politics. Unlike earlier protests rooted in ideology or policy, this one questions the BJP's ability to govern. "They must recalibrate their strategy," Verma says.Many of those protesting, he adds, are not natural opponents of the BJP. They are "aspirational young Indians and their parents who see these exams as a path to upward mobility". Because the grievance is about governance rather than ideology, he argues, the BJP cannot easily rally its supporters or deploy the political counter-narratives that helped it confront earlier protest movements.Image source, ReutersImage caption, An injured teenage protester rests in her mother's lap at Jantar Mantar, a day after thousands marched on parliament over exam paper leaks in DelhiMonday's demonstrations appeared to reinforce that assessment. For the past two weeks, comparisons with the anti-corruption movement of 2011 had largely centred on the CJP's impressive online reach, while sceptics questioned whether digital momentum could spill onto the streets. Monday's turnout suggested it could.Joyojit Pal, a professor at the University of Michigan who studies technology and society, says social media has become "a tinderbox waiting to explode with the next thing", its speed and reach driven by emotion.Long-simmering frustration over the state's lack of accountability created the conditions for this "implosion", he told the BBC, adding that the architecture of social media makes such eruptions likely to recur.How exam scandals threaten the future of India's young peopleThe question now is whether that online surge has become something more enduring."I think Monday's gathering indicates this movement may have crossed a tipping point," Verma says. Whether it grows from here, he adds, will depend largely on how the government responds.For Aiyar, Monday's protests revealed something equally significant. Student unions, Left groups and organisations such as the Bhim Army, a Dalit rights group, were present, but they were ultimately "outnumbered by an organic outpouring of students from all over the country"."That's unique compared to any significant protest in India's recent history," she says. Image source, Hindustan Times via Getty ImagesImage caption, Cockroach Janata Party founder Abhijeet Dipke during a sit-in at Jantar Mantar demanding the education minister's resignationJust as significantly, Aiyar believes the movement has already outgrown the organisation that sparked it and Wangchuk's hunger strike."The catalyst may have been the CJP," she says, "but it became bigger than them."Students came not only because of the state's handling of the protests, she argues, but because they wanted "a space and a site to express the deep frustration" they had been carrying for some time. "The memes, posters and slogans suggested grievances extending well beyond exam paper leaks."After initially leaving the response to ministers and party spokespersons, the  ruling BJP party's top leadership stepped in on Wednesday. Defence Minister Rajnath Singh accused the opposition of using students "as a political tool" while insisting the government remained committed to addressing their concerns through parliament. Modi addressed the issue publicly for the first time on Thursday."Nothing is more important than the welfare and future of our youth," he wrote on X, announcing fast-track courts to ensure "swift and stringent punishment" for those involved in paper leaks. "Those who try to harm the future of our youth will not be spared."Whether those assurances will be enough to defuse the protests remains uncertain. Aiyar believes the movement has tapped into broader anxieties over a slowing economy, scarce jobs and a deepening sense that the future offers diminishing opportunities.Image source, AFP via Getty ImagesImage caption, Many believe the ongoing protests have tapped into wider public frustrations"There is deep economic dissatisfaction and distress, which has translated into a lack of hope about the future," Aiyar says. "This could reflect growing exhaustion with a government that has been in power for a long time. Its failure to deliver on the promises it built its reputation on may have reached a tipping point."For her, the protests are about far more than a single policy failure. "The message from protesters is clear: we're exhausted. We need a new kind of politics, a new political grammar - one rooted in ideas and focused on governance and delivery."Whether that frustration can be sustained as a political movement, however, is another question. Political scientist Suhas Palshikar argues the Cockroach movement may simply provide an outlet for public anger before gradually losing steam, much like the farmers' protests."Where will the Cockroach movement go? Perhaps nowhere - it will allow venting of frustration and taper off," he wrote on X. He also cautions against romanticising it as a Gen Z uprising. "It would be a mistake to imagine a youth movement or a Gen Z movement - these are only convenient myths for commentators." The movement's "ideological and political character... is currently fluid", he argues, with different political forces likely to seek influence over it.What the protesters have undeniably shown, he says, is a willingness to confront the state. "The young have shown their angst and their disgust. They have borne the brunt of the democratic government's brutal police. They have also shown that only street action makes this regime... listen and talk."Whether that energy can endure, Palshikar argues, will depend on many factors, including state repression, media blackouts, leadership challenges and the difficulty of building a broad social coalition. Economist Surajit Mazumdar of Delhi's Jawaharlal Nehru University also believes the movement already points to something much larger than examination paper leaks.Image source, AFP via Getty ImagesImage caption, Students participating in India's anti-corruption movement in 2011"The current protest and the spontaneous response it has evoked is indicative of the widespread discontent of young people and students about the state of affairs in the country and in the education system which goes beyond the specific paper leak issue," he told the BBC. Compared with earlier student mobilisations, he argues, the current movement appears "broader and larger in scale".Mazumdar also rejects the idea that the protests are post-ideological. Like the farmers' movement before them, he says they show that "the democratic spirit is still alive in Indian society". For Aiyar, that democratic impulse is already reshaping politics."People are seeking accountability for governance in sites that are non-electoral," Aiyar says. "The split between electoral legitimacy and democratic accountability outside electoral space is the new phenomenon of politics in India."Whether that proves to be the defining legacy of the Cockroach Janta Party's protests - or merely a passing moment of public anger - will become clear only with time.

schema.headline: CJP protests at Jantar Mantar: The youth movement India's Modi didn't see coming

title: Novel analysis reveals playful side of Alan Turing
description: Dillon said she believed the story was a direct fictionalisation of a sexual encounter in the 1950s which led to Turing's arrest.Turing was convicted
body: Dillon said she believed the story was a direct fictionalisation of a sexual encounter in the 1950s which led to Turing's arrest.Turing was convicted of gross indecency in 1952 and forced by authorities to take hormones.He took his own life in June 1954.Turing was pardoned in 2013 and four years later, the government announced gay and bisexual men convicted of now-abolished sexual offences in England and Wales would also receive posthumous pardons under what was known as Turing's Law."Turing died when he was only 41, and in those years running up to his death, he was trying to understand himself and his world," Dillon added."Literature offered him a really powerful way of doing that. "This was the moment when he really felt a release, a chance to be more creative and playful."Dillon also found that Turing copied the style of a gay novel, Hemlock And After, by fellow Bletchley Park veteran Angus Wilson.She argued it was the novel Turing needed to read at a pivotal moment in his life, adding that it must have been a "powerful moment for Turing amid so much trauma".Do you have a story suggestion? Contact us below.

schema.headline: Novel analysis reveals playful side of Alan Turing

title: Decoding Charli XCX's new album, Music, Fashion, Film
description: Charli XCX was supposed to be taking a break.Her sixth album, Brat, was the sort of record you could genuinely call era-defining, without being accuse
body: Charli XCX was supposed to be taking a break.Her sixth album, Brat, was the sort of record you could genuinely call era-defining, without being accused of overstating its significance. Experimental but accessible songs like Guess, Apple and 360 propelled the star to new heights of fame. Her album was co-opted into Kamala Harris's 2024 presidential campaign, and Collins English Dictionary updated its definition of Brat, to acknowledge its use as an adjective characterising the music's "confident, independent, and hedonistic attitude". After a punishing year-long tour, the singer accepted a few acting roles, wrote the soundtrack to Emerald Fennel's adaptation of Wuthering Heights and married her longterm partner George Daniel.But instead of settling down, she found herself back in the studio."Out of the blue, inspiration struck me," she says. "I suddenly felt like I had something to say, so I ran with it."In a matter of weeks, she dashed off 11 short, guitar-based songs that tried to make sense of her whirlwind two years.The resulting album, titled Music, Fashion, Film, is smart, self-referential, funny, antagonistic and crammed full of moments to stimulate your inner earworm.Significantly, deliberately, it's not Brat 2.0. "If I'd made another album that felt more dance-leaning, it would have felt really hard, really sad," she told Vogue in the first interview for the album, external."To be boring is to die right there on the spot," she added in a newsletter to fans, external. "Give up, give in, go home, stay home, end it all."As a result, there's a lot to uncover in the music and lyrics. Here's what we learned.In the aforementioned Vogue article, Charli played the interviewer a track called Rock Music, featuring the lyric: "I think the dancefloor is dead, so now we're making rock music".The publication took that as gospel, calling Music, Fashion, Film the star's "rock reinvention". Fans recoiled. Courtney Love accused Charli of being a troll. Madonna captioned a photo, "If your dance floor feels dead, maybe you're playing the wrong music."But while guitars are the album's predominant sound, it's far from being a rejection of club music. Even the song Rock Music is full of manipulated vocals and electronic flourishes."I never said I was making a rock album," Charli posted on Instagram, explaining that the song was really about her relationship with the Brat album. "I've made electronic-leaning music for over a decade," she told music YouTuber Nicky Reardon. , external"That's shaped who I am as an artist. [Rock Music] is kind of about my pivot from that as an artist who doesn't want to continue to do the same thing over and over and over again."Rather than a picture of Charli, the album is being promoted with a stylish black-and-white photo of Velvet Underground musician John Cale, fashion designer Marc Jacobs, and film director Martin Scorsese.The trio epitomise excellence in their respective fields - reflecting the album's overarching theme of art, excellence and immortality.The photo was taken in New York this April, by Scottish filmmaker Aidan Zamiri, who's been behind the camera for some of Charli's recent music videos, as well as her mockumentary The Moment."Honestly it was a really emotional day," the singer told BBC 6 Music's Nick Grimshaw, external. "I was like, 'Wow, these three people mean so much to me personally as an artist'. And I couldn't believe that they were all together, you know? "We were so nervous and then when it was happening it was just honestly really sweet."On Rock Music, Charli admits that the head-banging physicality of the Brat tour ended up giving her nerve damage in her neck."It really impacts a lot on my sleep habits," she told Sam Murphy on the Pop Pod, external, saying the pain had got "progressively worse over the past couple of years". "When I think about touring this record I do think I should probably be taking a different approach," she added. "But who knows where I'll end up on that front, you know? Sometimes I just can't stop myself."Fans got a glimpse of how things might change at a secret show in London last week, where Charli performed some of her new material for the first time.Flanked by a tight, three piece band, the set was less demanding from a body-slamming perspective, without sacrificing the energy of the music.Reviews weren't allowed - but let's just say the new material, with its distorted guitars and punchy drums, sounded even better live.Charli's new songs find her grappling with whether she can stay true to her pugnacious, subversive roots while enjoying commercial success and personal happiness.The slinky single SS26 discusses the pressure on pop stars to do and say the right things. Lyrically, Charli disavows cigarettes and writes sincere apologies for her past mistakes. She even talks about using her politic as a "press strategy", noting that her part-Indian heritage "could give me quite the USP".Laced with sarcasm, it highlights the idiocy of holding celebrities to unattainable standards, instead of letting music (and fashion and film) explore the darker side of human nature. "If you're on the internet, there's this feeling like you need to be on the right side of every single conversation," Charli told Nicky Reardon. "You can't just be good. You have to be the right type of good, in a specific way and if not, you're a bad person who actually had a sinister motive this entire time. So the song is actually talking about my experience with that."Equally compelling is the single Wink Wink, where Charli emphasises her newfound maturity - wearing trousers, driving sensible cars - before admitting it's all a pose."I'm not a bad girl anymore / Wink, wink."But the most nakedly honest lyric comes on I'm Afraid - potentially the least romantic song ever written about getting engaged."Happiness came in a box," Charli says of the proposal, before spiralling into a panic about who she'll become if she says "yes"."I'm a wife, I'm a kid / I'm destroyed, I'm a bitch / I'm too drunk, I can't sleep / I can't feel, I'm afraid / I'm nothing without him / What if I ruin it?" she sings, as discordant synths rip apart the sonic fabric.Connecting the songs is the idea that everyone's flawed - but the mess and the friction make life more interesting.If the lyrics on Brat were direct and unembellished, Charli's new album takes minimalism to an extreme.The seventh song, Yeah, is a textbook example of tension and release in pop music - that strips the idea down to its most elemental form. For the first 30 seconds, Charli simply repeats the phrase, "I don't wanna," over and over while a guitar chugs in the background.Then, just when you're about to hit the off switch, she declares: "Wait! Now I wanna feel it," and the music explodes into life.It's so stupid and basic, and yet it scratches a tried-and-tested musical itch with such precision that you have to admire its audacity.Yeah is the most demo-like song on a 29-minute album that occasionally feels under-cooked. But the brevity was quite deliberate."I wasn't like, 'Oh, I must make an album with tiny songs'," Charli told Nick Grimshaw. "That was just the way that the songs came out. It was just a choice that felt right for the music that we were making."Charli is an avowed film buff, whose fifth album was named after David Cronenberg's notorious body-horror film Crash.The Canadian director is also the last voice you hear on Music, Fashion, Film, in an excerpt of an "hour-long" conversation he had with Charli last year,."What's crazy is he actually spoke in tune with the song, without ever having heard it," she told Australia's Triple J radio station., externalIn the clip, Cronenberg talks about the legacy of art, and whether longevity really matters. "Yes, some artists have created art that has really survived for thousands of years,  and that's fantastic," he says. "But experientially, for them, they are dead. They are gone. They're not experiencing that."That's not a cause for sadness, he insists. It means you shouldn't be burdened by the success (or failure) of your work."Oblivion is freedom."It feels like he's showing Charli the escape route from post-Brat expectations. On the evidence of this album, she's already made it through the door.

schema.headline: Decoding Charli XCX's new album, Music, Fashion, Film

title: Joshua vs Prenga: The 24 hours which will make or break British super-fight
description: The two days which could make or break Fury-AJFigure caption, You can be a hero or a coward. I'm trying to be the hero - JoshuaByKal SajadBBC Sport bo
body: The two days which could make or break Fury-AJFigure caption, You can be a hero or a coward. I'm trying to be the hero - JoshuaByKal SajadBBC Sport boxing journalistAfter more than a decade of posturing and power struggles - a soap opera of warring promoters, shifting managers, fractured television deals and failed negotiations - it has finally come down to this.The biggest fight in British boxing history - Tyson Fury versus Anthony Joshua - hangs on one final two-day stretch.On Friday, Fury, 37, fights Mariusz Wach in the unlikely surroundings of Pattaya, Thailand. There is no broadcaster, only a modest crowd expected and a 46-year-old opponent.The following evening, almost 4,000 miles away in Jeddah, Saudi Arabia, Joshua returns against Kristian Prenga - an Albanian knockout artist whom even the most hardened boxing fans would struggle to pick out of a line-up.If all goes to plan, Fury will leave Thailand within hours of beating Wach, board a private jet and fly to Saudi Arabia, where he is expected to climb through the ropes and finally stand face-to-face with Joshua.The contracts are signed but, especially in boxing, the ink is never truly dry until the opening bell rings.Why Joshua needed this warm-up fightFury and Joshua, 36, have defined British heavyweight boxing for more than a decade without ever sharing a professional ring.Yet they arrive at this moment for very different reasons. For Joshua, it is about far more than shaking off ring rust.The two-time world champion has not fought since surviving the car crash in Nigeria last December that claimed the lives of close friends Sina Ghami and Latif "Latz" Ayodele.Joshua has spoken about the emotional toll of the tragedy. Promoter Eddie Hearn believes boxing has "saved" Joshua - just as it did when he first took up the sport as a troubled 19-year-old searching for direction."When you come off an incident like that and an accident like that, I guess people say, 'Will you ever be the same?' Certainly everything he's shown in training camp, after he went through all the near-impossible work to get himself in that position, shows that he is," Hearn told BBC Sport."The questions that you ask yourself, the moments where your head hits the pillow at night and it's just you in your room, what's going on inside between those two ears?"There was also the physical recovery."In the early stages, just a slight movement in bed would be excruciating pain," Hearn said."To getting back on the bike and doing two hours' cycling when he couldn't move his upper body, and going through the physio sessions, that was an incredible journey."Fury's warm-up about publicity, not preparationFury, who briefly retired after back-to-back defeats by Oleksandr Usyk, already had the comeback fight many felt he needed when comfortably outpointing Arslanbek Makhmudov in April.BBC boxing pundit Steve Bunce is not convinced another outing was necessary."The fight against Makhmudov was perfect, it was the dream fight for a fighter after a long break," Bunce says."You could see he wasn't bothered about the stoppage, he just needed those priceless rounds. But this fight against Wach is one he will not lose under any circumstances. It's ceremonial."Fury's trip to Thailand appears to be as much about publicity as preparation.The cameras for the third season of his Netflix documentary, 'At Home with the Furys' will be rolling at ringside with exclusive access. No other broadcaster will be present.Will they both win and is Fury-AJ at risk?Figure caption, AJ, Hearn & Smith talk Prenga, Fury and becoming undisputedIt would rank among the biggest upsets in heavyweight history if Fury lost to a man who turns 47 this year and has lost seven of his past 10 fights."Fury should dominate but the biggest risk for me is that he looks awful," Bunce says."Also, Tyson might find out that he has lost something and senses some flaws in himself."There is a fraction more jeopardy for Joshua. Prenga is largely an unknown quantity to the wider public, but his record demands respect: 20 wins, 20 knockouts and one defeat."This fight is designed to be competitive, but not too competitive," Hearn says."He's never boxed at AJ's level, but until you fight at that level, you actually don't know if you're good enough. But he's decent, he's game and he's dangerous."The greater threat to the Fury-AJ super-fight is injury."Tyson is too smart and clever and I think Wach won't be using his elbows or head. There will be some sort of gentleman's agreement," Bunce adds."He could injure his arm or leg. At Tyson's age, with hard training camps, that can happen. It could also happen to Joshua."Will Fury take a private jet to Jeddah? Where will Fury-AJ land?If both men emerge unscathed, attention will quickly turn to Jeddah. The hope is that Fury will travel straight from Thailand and join Joshua in the ring for an official announcement."Perhaps Fury will get on a private jet and arrive in Jeddah - give it a bit of drama in the style of WWE," Bunce said."But if they both win, you have to announce it right then. You can't let it slip."Remarkably, the two have only ever shared a ring once before - a semi-mythical sparring session back in 2010 when Fury was a 21-year-old prospect with an undefeated professional record and Joshua was a raw 20-year-old amateur.Hearn is a little less certain Fury will make the journey to Saudi Arabia."We hope, now that the contracts are signed, we'll see him on Saturday," Hearn said."I would suggest most people would get on a plane, but he is Tyson Fury."Saudi Arabia powerbroker Turki Alalshikh, a key figure in bringing together Fury v Joshua, has said that for the fight to happen on British soil, it would require a middle-of-the-night start time at Wembley Stadium to cater for prime-time American audiences on Netflix.Such a plan would require extending Wembley's usual 11pm curfew, although sources close to London Mayor Sadiq Khan indicated the city would be willing to help make it happen.Industry whispers point towards Las Vegas or New York, while Hearn says Joshua's contract stipulates the fight must happen in the UK - but he has not ruled out a move to America.The prospect of the biggest British fight in history taking place thousands of miles away is a difficult one for many UK fans to accept.Whatever unfolds across these extraordinary coming hours, we are about to find out whether the biggest fight in British boxing history is going to be a reality or destined to remain the sport's greatest what-if.

schema.headline: Joshua vs Prenga: The 24 hours which will make or break British super-fight

title: Hero swimmer helped two children at West Mersea beach tragedy
description: Witnesses say a heroic day-tripper managed to haul two children back to shore during an incident where a mother and daughter drowned.Beverly Smit, 60,
body: Witnesses say a heroic day-tripper managed to haul two children back to shore during an incident where a mother and daughter drowned.Beverly Smit, 60, described the unnamed rescuer swimming out to sea three times as the tragedy unfolded in West Mersea, near Colchester, on Wednesday evening.Shally Rahman, 41, and Samiha Rahman, 15, were trying to rescue a seven-year-old boy from the water when they got into difficulty and died.Essex Police said the group, from Barking, east London, were all related to each other and the boy remained in hospital, fighting for his life.Recalling the moment the "Good Samaritan" sprang into action, Smit said: "They were calling for help and the children and adults were out there."He swam out, I think, three times. Two of the children he brought back were not in good condition."It's not an easy swim when the tide's coming in - you're swimming against the tide. The water [on Wednesday] was particularly quite cold."

schema.headline: Hero swimmer helped two children at West Mersea beach tragedy
